_meta.notes: Ch3 L2. luto, magluto, nagluto. mag-/nag- prefix introduced.

cards.body: Luto means 'cook.' It's a root word, just like kain. And just like kain, you add something to it to make a verb.
Add mag- to the front and it becomes magluto: 'to cook' (present/future).

example.note: mag- goes in front, unlike um- which goes inside. Two different verb patterns.

cards.body: Change mag- to nag- and it becomes past tense: nagluto = cooked.

example.note: mag- = present/future. nag- = past. Same root, different prefix.

cards.explanation: Nag- = past tense. Nagluto = already cooked.

cards.body: You now know two ways to make verbs. um- goes inside (kumain). mag- goes in front (magluto). Different roots use different patterns. You'll learn which is which as you go.

cards.source_text: Nanay is going to cook. How do you say 'cook' (future)?
cards.explanation: Magluto = to cook / will cook. mag- prefix.
Two verb patterns: um- goes inside, mag- goes in front. nag- is past tense of mag-.
Nagluto ako = I cooked. Nag- = past tense.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: mag-/nag- is the most common verb pattern in Tagalog. Once you master it, you can conjugate hundreds of words.
In Taglish, affixes are ALWAYS Tagalog but the root can be English. 'Nag-cook ako' is natural Taglish. 'I nagluto' is not.
Adobo, sinigang, and tinola are the holy trinity of Filipino cooking. You'll learn these dishes soon.
